title: Nuclear Fuel Cycle — AI-Nuclear PPAs Lock In Multi-Decade Uranium Demand
summary: Hyperscalers have signed 9.7+ GW of nuclear PPAs, locking in 60+ years of fuel demand. Own the only Western full-cycle uranium house (CCJ) and the only US uranium enricher (LEU).
catalyst: On the one-year anniversary of President Trump's nuclear executive orders (May 2026), DOE announced an additional $94M in awards to eight nuclear supply-chain companies and confirmed release of 20 metric tons of HALEU into a fuel bank earmarked for AI infrastructure projects [5][8]. This sits on top of January 2026's $900M DOE task order to Centrus to expand the Piketon, Ohio enrichment plant — the only US-owned enrichment facility [4][7]. With Cameco reporting Q1 2026 adjusted EBITDA up 44% YoY to $509M [3] and term uranium prices at $93/lb (the highest since 2008), the fuel cycle is the bottleneck the equity market is still under-pricing relative to the generators.
thesis: The AI-nuclear PPA wave (Microsoft-Constellation 837 MW, Amazon-Talen 1.92 GW, Meta-Constellation 1.1 GW, Google-Kairos 500 MW [6]) creates 60-year fuel demand that cannot be substituted, deferred, or engineered away. Power-gen names (CEG, VST, GEV) have re-rated 2-4x on this thesis; the upstream fuel cycle has lagged despite Cameco controlling ~15% of global uranium output and Centrus operating the only US enrichment capacity. As utilities convert spot-market dependence into long-term contracts, term price ($93/lb) leading spot ($85/lb) confirms structural under-contracting — a classic pre-cycle signature [9][10].
body_md:
## Setup

The nuclear renaissance has gone from analyst talking-point to contracted reality in 18 months. Per SMR Intel's deal tracker, hyperscalers have committed to at least 13 nuclear power purchase agreements totaling 9.7+ GW since late 2024 [6]. Microsoft restarted Three Mile Island (837 MW, 20-year PPA with Constellation, online 2028). Amazon took 1.92 GW from Talen's Susquehanna plant in a 17-year PPA with ~$20B of associated investment. Meta locked up 1.1 GW from Constellation's Clinton plant. Google backed Kairos Power with a 500 MW SMR development deal targeting 2030 [6].

Every one of those gigawatts requires fuel. Forever. A typical 1 GW light-water reactor consumes ~150-200 metric tons of natural uranium and ~25-30 metric tons of enriched uranium product per year. SMRs use HALEU (5-20% enriched) — a fuel that, until 2025, was produced commercially only in Russia. The Trump administration's May 2025 executive orders made re-shoring this supply chain a national security priority, and one year in, the policy is converting into contracted dollars.

## Why Now

Three dated catalysts compress the timeline:

1. **DOE one-year EO anniversary (May 2026)**: White House Director Michael Kratsios announced $94M in fresh awards to eight nuclear supply-chain companies and confirmed release of 20 MT of HALEU from the DOE fuel bank for AI-adjacent reactors [5][8]. This is small in absolute dollars but signals continued policy compounding — the administration is using executive authority to clear permitting, not just appropriate money.

2. **Centrus $900M Piketon task order (Jan 2026)**: DOE awarded Centrus' American Centrifuge Operating subsidiary a $900M task order to expand the Piketon, Ohio facility to commercial-scale HALEU and LEU production [4][7]. This is the only US-owned enrichment capacity. Centrus simultaneously disclosed a commercial LEU backlog of $2.3B and a 12 MT/yr HALEU build-out plan [4].

3. **Cameco Q1 2026 print (May 2026)**: Revenue $845M (+7% YoY), net earnings $131M (+87%), adjusted EBITDA $509M (+44%), with both the uranium segment and the Westinghouse JV contributing [3]. Management reaffirmed 2026 production of 19.5-21.5M lbs and a contract book averaging 28M lbs/year through 2030 [3]. Long-term uranium contract price hit $93/lb, the highest since 2008 [9][10].

The spot price (~$85/lb) is now meaningfully below the term price (~$93/lb). Historically that inversion has been a precursor to large utility re-contracting waves as buyers move to lock in supply before scarcity gets priced in.

## Company-Specific Edge

### CCJ — Cameco Corporation (IOWN score 74, HOLD, Growth sleeve)

Cameco is the only Western pure-play covering the entire fuel cycle: ~15% of global uranium mine production via tier-1 Canadian and Kazakh assets, conversion (UO2/UF6), fuel fabrication, and — since the 2023 Brookfield JV — a 49% stake in Westinghouse, the leading Western reactor designer and services franchise. The Westinghouse leg matters because it captures economics from new SMR builds and existing-reactor life extensions independent of uranium price.

Screener data points: revenue 5yr CAGR 23%, profit margin 17%, ROE 8.9%, forward P/E 60.9. AI Resilience score 9/10 — the business is structurally immune to AI substitution and is one of the cleanest pick-and-shovel demand beneficiaries. Forward P/E of 61 is expensive, but earnings power inflects with each multi-year contract Cameco signs at $90+/lb realized prices (versus a contract book historically averaging $50-60/lb).

### LEU — Centrus Energy (IOWN score 70, HOLD, Growth sleeve)

Centrus owns the only American uranium enrichment plant of any scale. That is not a commercial moat — it is a national security designation. The Russian HALEU import ban (Prohibiting Russian Uranium Imports Act, 2024) and the Inflation Reduction Act / nuclear-fuel-security funding ($3.4B authorized, $2.7B disbursed to date) effectively guarantee Centrus' addressable market. The January 2026 $900M Piketon task order is the single largest commercial-scale HALEU contract ever awarded [4].

Screener data points: revenue $448.7M (15% 3-yr CAGR), profit margin 17.3%, ROE 16.8%, forward P/E 47, AI Resilience score 10/10 [internal report]. Innovation score 8/10 reflects the fact that nobody else in North America can do what Centrus does, and capacity additions are 6-8 years out for any new entrant. The $2.3B commercial backlog is ~5x current revenue.

## Numbers That Matter

| Metric | CCJ | LEU |
|---|---|---|
| Stock price (May 18, 2026) | $111 | $207 |
| Forward P/E | 60.9 | 47.0 |
| ROE | 8.9% | 16.8% |
| Profit margin | 16.9% | 17.3% |
| Revenue growth (5yr CAGR / 3yr) | 23% | 15% |
| AI Resilience (IOWN /10) | 9 | 10 |
| Contract backlog | 28M lbs/yr through 2030 | $2.3B commercial LEU |
| Q1 2026 adj EBITDA | $509M (+44% YoY) | n/d |

Market-wide demand: World Nuclear Association projects uranium demand rising from ~67,000 tU in 2024 to ~87,000 tU by 2030 (+28%) and ~150,000 tU by 2040 (+120%) [2]. Global nuclear capacity nearly doubles from 398 GW to 746 GW by 2040 [2]. Hyperscaler 2026 AI capex of $725B is the demand-side accelerant that turns the WNA reference scenario into the high case.

## Bear Case

1. **Term price plateau**: If utility contracting slows because $93/lb is already considered a structural ceiling, Cameco's realized prices on new contracts disappoint. The Kazakh state miner Kazatomprom has been quietly increasing supply guidance.

2. **SMR delays**: Most contracted SMR capacity is 2028-2032 first-fuel. NRC permitting friction or first-of-a-kind cost overruns at Kairos, X-energy, or NuScale could push the demand curve right by 2-3 years. HALEU demand is the longest-duration leg of the thesis.

3. **Centrus customer concentration**: DOE is currently Centrus' largest customer; if the program is reorganized under a future administration, the runway compresses. Mitigant: the underlying utility contracts are 7-10 year takes from regulated operators, not the federal government.

4. **Valuation**: CCJ at 61x forward EPS and LEU at 47x forward EPS both require multi-year earnings inflection to work. Neither is a cheap stock today.

## Conclusion

The AI-power trade has migrated from chips → generation → fuel. The market has paid up for the first two legs (NVDA, CEG, VST, GEV) but is under-positioned in the fuel cycle where contract structure converts AI capex into multi-decade revenue visibility. CCJ and LEU are the two cleanest ways to own that. Both screen as HOLD on absolute IOWN scores but read as High Conviction in this specific second-order context: 9.7 GW of signed PPAs that, by physics, require uranium and enrichment they cannot source from anyone else in the West.

ticker_rationale.CCJ: Cameco's Q1 2026 adjusted EBITDA grew 44% YoY to $509M on a contract book averaging 28M lbs/yr through 2030 [3]. The Westinghouse JV adds a non-uranium-price-linked services franchise. AI Resilience 9/10, 23% 5-yr revenue CAGR per the IOWN screener.
ticker_rationale.LEU: Centrus owns the only US uranium enrichment plant and just won a $900M DOE task order to expand Piketon [4]. Commercial LEU backlog is $2.3B vs $449M revenue — ~5x book-to-bill. AI Resilience 10/10, 16.8% ROE per the IOWN screener.

risks: Kazatomprom or Russian Rosatom resume aggressive supply growth, capping uranium term prices below $90/lb and slowing utility re-contracting velocity
SMR first-of-a-kind delays (Kairos, X-energy, NuScale) push HALEU demand out by 2-3 years, compressing Centrus' near-term backlog conversion
A future administration reorganizes DOE nuclear fuel security funding, removing the largest current customer for Centrus' HALEU production
Both names trade at 47-61x forward EPS — multiple compression on any AI capex disappointment hits these stocks harder than the generators
invalidation: Long-term uranium contract price (TradeTech LT indicator) falls below $80/lb for two consecutive monthly prints, signaling utility contracting wave is reversing
Cameco reports a 2026 quarterly contract book reduction (currently 28M lbs/yr through 2030) or guides Westinghouse JV earnings contribution down by >25%
Centrus loses or sees a >30% reduction in its DOE HALEU task order ($900M baseline), or cancels the 12 MT/yr Piketon expansion plan
A major hyperscaler publicly walks away from a nuclear PPA (e.g., Microsoft-Constellation TMI, Amazon-Talen Susquehanna), breaking the AI-nuclear demand narrative
